Call 2024 Round 3 YOU
KA152-YOU - Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-76267760 Deadline (Brussels Time) 08 Oct 2024 12:00:00
Project Summary
Please provide short answers to the following questions to summarise the information you have provided in the rest of the
application form.
Please use full sentences and clear language. In case your project is accepted, the summary you provided will be made
public by the European Commission and the National Agencies.
What do you want to achieve by implementing the project? What are the objectives of your project? Please specify from the
perspective of youth work practice.
The objectives of the "Unleashing Your Creative Voice through Media" project are multifaceted, encompassing both short-
term and long-term goals from the perspective of youth work practice. Through the implementation of this project, we aim to
achieve the following :
Skill Development:
One of the primary objectives is to equip young participants with practical skills in media production, journalism, and digital
storytelling. By offering a diverse range of workshops and hands-on activities, we seek to enhance participants' proficiency
in various media formats, including blogging, video production, and podcasting. Through activities such as the "Basics of
Journalism" workshop and the "Role-play Interviews," participants will develop essential skills such as writing, reporting,
interviewing, and public speaking, crucial for personal and professional development.
Critical Thinking and Media Literacy:
Another key objective is to promote critical thinking and media literacy among participants. In an era of information overload
and misinformation, it is essential for young people to be able to discern credible information from misinformation and to
critically evaluate media content. Activities such as the "Media Detective" and "Content Critique and Feedback Rounds" are
designed to enhance participants' ability to analyse media content, identify biases, and engage in informed public discourse.
Intercultural Understanding and European Identity:
From a European perspective, the project aims to promote intercultural understanding and cultivate a sense of European
citizenship and identity among participants. By bringing together young adults from diverse cultural backgrounds and
facilitating cross-cultural exchanges, we aim to foster a sense of solidarity and cooperation among participants. Through
teamwork-based media projects that reflect diverse cultures and perspectives, participants will develop a deeper
appreciation for Europe's cultural diversity and shared heritage, contributing to the broader goal of building a cohesive and
inclusive European community.
Empowerment and Active Citizenship:
Ultimately, the overarching objective of the project is to empower young participants to become proactive creators of media
content and engaged citizens in their communities and societies. By providing them with the skills, knowledge, and
experiences necessary to navigate the digital landscape ethically and effectively, we aim to empower participants to make
meaningful contributions to their communities, advocate for positive change, and actively participate in democratic
processes. Through activities such as the "Mock Press Conference" and "Final Reflection and Output Presentation,"
participants will have opportunities to apply their skills and knowledge in real-world contexts, gaining confidence and agency
as active citizens.
Through the achievement of these objectives, we aspire to facilitate the personal and professional development of
participants and contribute to the creation of a more inclusive, informed, and democratic society.
What activities do you plan to implement? What is the number and profile of the participants involved?
The project’s main activity will be the youth exchange that will happen in 2025 July in Balatonkeresztúr.
The activities planned for the "Unleashing Your Creative Voice through Media" youth exchange consists a diverse range of
workshops, interactive sessions, and teamworking aimed at empowering young participants with practical skills in media
production, journalism, and digital storytelling. The activities are following a structure and every day of the activity has a
specific thematic.
They are tailored for 35 young adults aged between 18 and 30, from 5 different countries and different backgrounds, with 6
participants (+1 group leader) per country. Each group of participants from every country will be accompanied by a group
leader, who must be at least 18 years old and have experience in youth work and/or Erasmus+ projects. All participants
possess at least B1 level of English proficiency. They are enthusiastic about engaging in media and journalism-related
activities, demonstrating high motivation to participate. Additionally, they are eager to embark on this new experience
abroad to expand their knowledge on the topic.
The following is a list of the most important activities of the youth exchange:

KA152-YOU - Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-76267760 Deadline (Brussels Time) 08 Oct 2024 12:00:00
1. "Illustrate Your Introduction":
Participants will take part in an introduction game where they draw a word card and select an image to reveal something
about themselves, integrating elements of their native language and cultural experiences. This activity fosters a sense of
belonging and cultural exchange among participants.
2. Live news desk simulation
Participants will step into the shoes of presenters, reporters and producers. They are tasked with managing a flow of
incoming information, which includes prepared scenarios that simulate real reporting challenges. Participants will be divided
into groups, each of which will have to create a news flow (3 breaking news stories) on 3 different themes that they have
chosen.
3. "Expectation & Commitment Tree":
In this group activity, participants express their motivations, expectations, and commitments using colour-coded paper
shapes. By visually mapping out their thoughts, participants gain a deeper understanding of their own motivations and those
of their peers, fostering a sense of community and shared purpose.
4. Self-made Code Names - Custom Media Edition:
Taking a leaf out of the Code Names game, participants create a similar game focus on the media industry. They generate
lists of media-related words independently, then collaborate in groups to expand these lists. After exchanging lists with other
teams, they play the game following standard rules, with teams guessing words based on clues from their spymaster. This
activity promotes teamwork, creativity, and knowledge of media terms.
5. Basics of Journalism:
This workshop provides theoretical and practical training on news writing, research techniques, and ethical standards in
journalism.. Participants learn the fundamentals of journalistic practices and engage in hands-on activities to develop their
skills in gathering and presenting information.
6. Media Detective - Clues and cues:
Participants work in teams to investigate news snippets, images, and statements, honing their fact-checking and critical
analysis skills. Through collaborative research and discussion, participants learn to discern credible information from
misinformation, promoting media literacy and critical thinking.
7. The News Factory:
"The News Factory" game, inspired by the Undercover game, deepens participants understanding of media production
complexities, focusing on combating fake news. Through roles of journalists and saboteurs, it enhances critical thinking
skills and awareness of misinformation tactics. The game fosters communication, teamwork, and ethical decision-making
vital for effective journalism, promoting media literacy and ethical practices. Participants gain empathy and insight into news
production's ethical stakes.
8. Story Circle:
Participants collaboratively build stories based on given themes, enhancing their narrative skills and understanding of
storytelling structures. Through active participation and creative collaboration, participants learn to craft engaging narratives
and explore different storytelling techniques.
9. Role-playing game: reporting news:
Participants engage in role-play scenarios as interviewers and interviewees, practising effective questioning and ethical
journalism practices. Through simulated interviews and feedback sessions, participants develop their interviewing skills and
learn to navigate ethical dilemmas in journalism.
10. Mock Press Conference:
In this simulation, participants develop public speaking skills and learn to manage real-time information dissemination. By
assuming roles as spokespersons and journalists, participants practise effective communication under pressure and learn
to respond to challenging questions with confidence and poise.
What results and impact do you expect your project to have?
In the short term, we anticipate progressive and quick results regarding digital literacy and practical multimedia production
skills among participants. They will gain a deeper understanding of media ethics and responsibility that is crucial for content
creation. Through collaborative learning experiences, we aim to promote cross-cultural cooperation and cultural diversity.
Additionally, participants will develop enhanced communication, public speaking, and interviewing skills, empowering them
to express themselves confidently on various topics through different media. These immediate impacts align with our
objectives of fostering critical media engagement and bridging the digital divide, making high-quality media training
accessible to diverse young adults.

Call 2024 Round 3 YOU
KA152-YOU - Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-76267760 Deadline (Brussels Time) 08 Oct 2024 12:00:00
Looking into the long term, we foresee a significant impact on participants' personal and professional development in the
field of media. By nurturing their creativity, innovation, and social responsibility, we aim to make our participants of proactive
media creators rather than passive consumers. This shift in mindset will not only benefit the individuals involved but also
contribute to their communities too. As participants continue to apply their acquired skills and knowledge, we expect to see a
ripple effect, with positive outcomes extending beyond the project's lifetime, namely participants applying skills from the
project in their workplaces, academic pursuits, and personal lives can positively impact teamwork, productivity, and digital
literacy among their peers, thereby extending the project's influence beyond its duration.
Moreover, by sharing their work on platforms accessible to a wider audience, such as social media channels associated
with the participating organisations, participants have the opportunity to reach and influence their community, including
followers of these organisations.
Furthermore, we anticipate broader societal impacts, including the promotion of European citizenship and identity through
collaborative media projects. By reflecting diverse cultures and perspectives, these projects have the potential to strengthen
bonds among European youth and foster a sense of belonging to a larger community. Moreover, by instilling ethical
journalism practices and promoting media literacy, we hope to combat the prevalence of misinformation and uphold the
integrity of information in society. This long-term impact aligns with our vision of empowering young adults to make
meaningful contributions to the digital age while fostering a sense of social responsibility and European unity. Overall, we
expect our project to leave a lasting legacy by equipping participants with the skills, knowledge, and values necessary to
navigate the complexities of the media landscape and make a positive impact on their communities and beyond.
Other expected outcomes include the establishment of lasting partnerships between participating organisations,
establishing for future collaborations and initiatives in media literacy and journalism between them.
Alongside these objectives, we also anticipate the broader impacts and outcomes typically associated with Erasmus+ youth
exchange programs, which significantly support both personal and professional growth. These includes, among others,
increased cultural understanding, promoting intercultural communication and dialogue, improving participants' key
competencies, and building soft skills through diverse non-formal learning approaches.

Please briefly present your organisation/the group.
Square of Youth Association (shortly: SOYA; in Hungarian: Fiatalok Tere Egyesület) is a diverse group of young people
brought together by Erasmus+ projects. Many of us have transformed our lives through these projects, developing key

Call 2024 Round 3 YOU
KA152-YOU - Mobility of young people (KA152-YOU)
Form ID KA152-YOU-76267760 Deadline (Brussels Time) 08 Oct 2024 12:00:00
competencies, soft skills, and forming connections with friends across Europe, among many other benefits. We are
dedicated to delivering youth work at a quality we would expect from ourselves, believing that the best way to engage in
youth work is through young people working with young people. We are truly an Erasmus+ generation at Square of Youth
Association.
Our mission is to connect Hungarian youth with Europe. We aim to provide opportunities for them to explore Europe,
connect with their European peers, and empower them to become active citizens. We encourage them to take initiatives and
contribute to their communities.
We also aim and working to:
- Help young people learn through non-formal learning activities and enhance their soft skills. Increasing youth participation
through our projects and providing international opportunities for young people.
- Connect youth with stakeholders and decision-makers, facilitating the translation of youth voices to them and increasing
youth involvement.
- Promote dialogue among young people from EU member states and candidate countries, which includes collaborating with
partners from outside the EU as well.
Internationality is a key component of our activities because we believe that international opportunities and mobilities have
the potential to improve young people's learning and growth on a high level.
We primarily focus on three key topics, but due to our diversity and ambition, we are open to many other areas as well. The
three key topics are:
- Entrepreneurship and empowering young people to become entrepreneurs. Several of our members became
entrepreneurs at a young age. We believe entrepreneurship not only as a business endeavor but also for its entrepreneurial
skills, which are great soft skills in many aspects of one’s life.
- Active citizenship and youth empowerment. Aiming for our projects and activities can have a multiplier effect and create
impact on a broader scale.
- Topics closely connected to the European Union and being a European citizen
SOYA is also a member of the Danube Youth Organization Network and is committed to fostering cooperation among
youth-focused organizations in the Danube Region. We find this particularly important in facilitating meetings with decision-
makers and establishing cross-border collaborations.
What are the activities and experience of the organisation in youth work? Please provide information on your organisation’s
/ group’s regular youth work activities.
All the activities we implement are thought, planned and realized by young people.
Regarding the topic of entrepreneurship, we have won the "Entrepreneur in a Week" Erasmus+ youth exchange project,
which will be implemented in spring 2025. Participants will receive all the necessary skills and knowledge to become
entrepreneurs in an intensive week, such as business plan writing, pitching, understanding how to get funding for their
ideas, and more. The project also focuses on developing entrepreneurial skills.
We are currently developing a Visegrad Minigen project: a policy hackathon aimed at young people to explore pathways to
entrepreneurship and identify necessary supports. The project will culminate in a policy document to be presented to
decision-makers and stakeholders.
Integration is also an important topic for us. Currently, we are implementing a Solidarity project in Budapest where we bring
Hungarian and international university students closer together through board games and regular local events.
We organize Erasmus+ information sessions with the goal of democratizing access to Erasmus+. Youth exchanges serve as
a valuable introduction for many young people, yet there are numerous other project types within Erasmus+—such as
Erasmus for Young Entrepreneurs or European Solidarity Corps (ESC)—that they may not be aware of.
In connection with this initiative, we were exhibiting at the Sziget Festival 2024 to engage naturally with young people,
speaking their language and promoting Erasmus+ and SOYA. Our goal is to attend more festivals to promote Erasmus+,
and non-formal learning, and to inform them about their opportunities.
In addition to our current projects, all of our members have previously participated in numerous Erasmus+ initiatives,
including youth exchanges, training courses, and Erasmus+ semesters at their university. Some of us have also taken part
in European Solidarity Corps volunteer programs, while others have engaged in Erasmus for Young Entrepreneurs. Before
choosing to become actively involved in the program, we were all well-integrated into the Erasmus community and deeply
passionate about its positive impact on our lives.

Please briefly present the organisation / the group
Move2Europe APS is a Non-profit/Non-Governmental Organisation based in southern Italy, precisely in the Campania
Region, District of Benevento.
It is a newborn association, but is the fruit of its founders' almost ten years' experience in the world of volunteering and non-
profit organisations, an experience made up of the promotion of culture and active citizenship, local and European projects,
Erasmus+ mobility, participation and inclusion, and an open approach to cultural, linguistic and social diversity.
MOVE2EUROPE APS is, therefore, an organisation that is fully anchored in its recent past and looking straight ahead into
the future. And it is so because it is founded on the values of citizenship without borders, of non-violence, of inclusion, of
integration, because it is based on the solid roots of its past but with the prospect of innovation always ahead of it.

organisation’s / group’s regular youth work activities at local level.
The staff of MOVE2EUROPE APS has a solid experience in organising: Training seminars for young people and for adults;
Training projects within the Erasmus+ program; audio-visual creation; Public events and initiatives; organization of sports
and tourism promotion events; Sending young people to Erasmus+ short-term mobility projects.
In recent years, MOVE2EUROPE APS staff members have also organised environmental activities (Plogging, ecological
events, nature photography competitions, etc.) in the area of Benevento district in order to raise awareness in the
communities of the district on issues related to biodiversity, environmental sustainability, as well as promote active
citizenship for young people.

ELVIRA SANTANIELLO – FOUNDER/PRESIDENT – YOUTH WORKER
Degree in Italian classic literature and culture (Cultural and archaeological heritage), has obtained a scholarship from the
Ministry of University and Scientific Research to attend the Advanced Training Course for Specialists in innovative practices
for the enhancement of cultural and environmental heritage.
Other skills:
• Web content experience for art, culture and volunteering on-line platform
• Social and cultural volunteer animator of young people, including those at risk or with disabilities
• 15 years’ experience as a professional children Animator (6-12years) and Team Manager: teaching labs, didactic tours,
organization activity.
• Diploma as Farm Holiday Expert: Voluntary Tour Guide for children/elderly experience
• Tourist tour planner experience: tourist itinerary projects, mainly focused on the approach of children to art.
Since 2006 full-time mum but still engaged in the social, political and cultural sphere. Collaborates with national and local
Associations and local committees in the organization of conferences, exhibitions, book presentation, meetings with the
authors et similia.
During the pandemic, organized and managed online awareness campaigns on the issue of feminicide and violence against
women in general, conducted online seminars on the Erasmus+ Program and the opportunities it offers, and organized
online meetings with authors, writers, and journalists.
Has created and managed a voluntary service to support the elderly, the sick, the frail people, and also children through
telephone readings of stories and fairy tales (this service is still ongoing).
Has implemented and still runs a creative writing workshop for children and kids and numerous writing contests.
Since 2018, she has been in charge of coordinating the Erasmus+ department of Koinokalo' APS and was the Head of
Koinokalò APS Sannio, a local branch of Koinokalo' APS in the inland areas of the Campania Region.
In this double role, has created a large network of collaborations with youth workers, educators and experts in various
subjects, in various parts of Italy and also abroad.
Since January 2024 she has been engaged as an activist in the anti-discrimination movement against body and weight
discrimination and manages a section on the association's website dedicated to Body Liberation.
STEFANO D’ERMILIO – VICE PRESIDENT -YOUTH LEADER
University student in computer science. He has taken part in numerous Youth Exchanges both as a participant and as a
Youth Leader.He is an athlete, with more than a decade of experience in Rugby. He is the coordinator of sports-themed

LUCIA GIACCIO - TECHNICAL ADVISER AND YOUTH LEADER
Biologist. PhD in Neuroscience (Neuropsychopharmacology and Clinical Toxicology), member of a research group studying
the effects of environmental pollution on health, author of several scientific publications in international journals. She has

Project Rationale
In this section, you are asked to explain the aims of your project, what you want to achieve and what are its expected
benefits on participants and participating organisations. Make sure to read the questions carefully and address all sub-
questions.
Needs and Objectives
Why do you want to carry out this project? Please describe the issues and needs you want to address and your project’s
objectives.
The project "Unleashing Your Creative Voice through Media" is driven by a multifaceted mission aimed at addressing critical
issues and needs prevalent among young Europeans in the digital age. At its core, the project seeks to empower young
adults with essential media literacy and production skills, responding to a pressing need highlighted by Eurostat, which
reveals that while 94% of 16-24 year-olds use the internet daily, only a fraction possess advanced digital skills. Besides, the
increase in fake news and propaganda in recent years underscores the necessity for young people to acquire skills enabling
them to distinguish truth from falsehood and recognize possible bias. This urgency is highlighted by the acknowledgment of
half of EU citizens aged between 15 and 30 that they require information and critical analysis skills to combat fake news.
However, promoting education in media and digital technologies at the secondary level faces obstacles such as the rapid
evolution of technologies, the swift dissemination of information, and a lack of teacher training in this domain . Therefore, our
project becomes crucial as it aims to empower young adults with media literacy skills, equipping them to navigate the
complex media landscape, critically evaluate information, and contribute to informed public discourse.
Moreover, our objectives extend beyond mere skill acquisition. We aim to empower young people to become proactive
creators of media content, transcending the role of passive consumers. By making high-quality media training accessible to
young adults from diverse backgrounds, we seek to bridge the digital divide and cultivate a sense of European citizenship
and identity through collaborative media projects that reflect diverse cultures and perspectives. Through non-formal
education methodologies and peer learning, we aspire to support personal and professional development in the field of
media, nurturing a generation of media-savvy individuals who are adept at navigating the complexities of the digital
landscape ethically and effectively.
Specifically, the project aims to empower young adults to express themselves confidently on topics close to their interests
through various media formats, ranging from blogging to podcasting. Participants will acquire fundamental skills in
journalism, including writing, reporting, and storytelling, while also enhancing their elocution and public speaking abilities.
They will be introduced to different mediums they can freely use and provided with hands-on training in creating
professional-quality videos and documentaries. Effective interviewing techniques for engaging with people on the streets
and capturing authentic stories will also be taught. Through these activities, participants will develop communication
strategies to effectively convey their message and engage their audience, while fostering creativity, innovation, and a sense
of social responsibility in media production and storytelling.
How does your project link to the objectives of the Erasmus programme and those of Youth Exchanges?
Our project is designed to align with the Erasmus programme (1), embodying EU values (2), and fulfilling the goals of Youth
Exchanges (3 & 4). It is committed to support intercultural dialogue, promoting non-formal education, and developing key
competences among young people. Below is a detailed analysis of how the project achieves these aims.
(1)Our project aligns with the objectives of the Erasmus Programme : Our project brings together participants from various
European countries, allowing them to discover different cultural perspectives, fostering mutual respect and cultural
awareness, a sense of European identity and broadening their understanding not only of various communication and
journalism topics, but also of English. It prioritises high-quality youth work, employing carefully structured activities like the
"Live News Desk Simulation" to help in developing practical skills in journalism and media. These activities utilize non-formal
education methods, engaging participants in the development process for an enriching and personalised learning
experience. By focusing on media and journalism, the project equips participants with valuable skills that enhance their
employability in today’s job market : critical thinking, public speaking, ethical journalism practices, and digital content
creation. In addition, the project actively promotes social inclusion and diversity by involving participants from different
countries and backgrounds. Activities are inclusive and accessible to all, ensuring full participation regardless of socio-
economic background, prior experience, or English proficiency.
(2)Our project embodies EU Values : it integrates discussions on ethical journalism and media integrity, emphasising the
importance of respecting human rights and dignity in media practices. Participants learn to balance freedom of expression
with the responsibility to report truthfully and ethically. Through activities like street interviews and the creation of policy
documents and games on media ethics, participants engage in practices that promote active citizenship. They learn to voice
their opinions, participate in democratic processes, and understand the role of free media in a democratic society by
discerning and discussing information. Besides, our activities balance individual involvement with collaborative work,
enabling participants to reach their potential while fostering solidarity and equality. Through collaborative projects and

In terms of skill acquisition, the project offers participants opportunities for personal and professional development that

The learning outcomes, especially the materials made by the participants, will be documented and disseminated through the
organizations websites and social media sites. We will share with other youth workers too throuh SALTO. This allows other
organisations, young people and everyone following these channels to learn from the project and contribute to the Erasmus+
community's collective knowledge on media literacy and non-formal education.
The connections established during the project extend beyond its immediate duration. Indeed, participants and
organisations remain in touch thanks to the WhatsApp group and follow-up meetings. These partnerships will allow us to
plan new ideas, initiatives, projects together after the project too. We are planning to build on the success of the project and
the lessons learnt.
To ensure the sustainability of the project's impact, follow-up activities on similar themes (for instance : joint projects or
physical or digital meetings with international partners, thematic workshops, quizzes on social media or through forms sent
per mail) are encouraged to be organised by the partner organizations and by the participants.
Last but not at least, our organization, Square of Youth Association is experienced as a partner organization but it’s still
awaiting to organize our first youth exchange. With this proejct, we can gain, as an organization, significant project
management, facilitation, community development skills which we can use in the future.
What would be the impact of your project beyond the participants and participating organisations, at local, regional,
national, if any European level ?
Regarding the local level: The participants will become ambassadors of Erasmus+. They will talk about it to their friends and
family, and they will share their experiences and what they have learned on social media. We will also encourage this
through mandatory dissemination. This will not only promote Erasmus+ but also the project's topic, and its positive impacts
may reach other young people who did not participate in the project.
By becoming more conscious media consumers, the participants will be able to spread these skills and knowledge within
their own communities, creating a multiplier effect with the project, which could lead to a long-term local impact.
Regarding regional impact: During the Budapest program, the participants will engage with locals, which will also help
promote the Erasmus+ program and our youth exchange in a natural way. The locals will have the opportunity to meet
people from different cultures and countries through these interactions.
It’s also important to mention that we will be using local ingredients and shopping from small local businesses during the
project, further supporting a positive impact on the community.
Regarding the European level: The materials created during the project will be uploaded to our website and the SALTO
platform. This will help European youth workers by providing inspiration, learning opportunities, and resources they can use
for their own projects. We will also encourage our partners to share these materials on their websites, which will extend the
project's impact across countries.
Thanks to our partnerships, we can achieve a long-term European impact, as these collaborations will not end with the
project but are intended for long-term cooperation. This will allow us to have a positive influence on young people across
different European countries and help in achieving the goals of Erasmus+.

Please describe the background of the participants in each participating group and how each group was formed. Please
also provide information on the group leaders, the age of the participants and how country balance is ensured. If
necessary, explain how the gender balance is respected.
Our project is designed to bring together young people across Europe. Participants will be carefully selected to ensure a
balanced and inclusive environment. In order to do so, we follow and respect certain criterias:
The youth exchange involves participants from four European countries (Hungary, Croatia, Poland, Italy, and Romania),
with each country contributing 7 participants, making a total of 35 participants. These countries are selected to ensure
regional diversity across Europe. Moreover, to increase country and regional balance, each partner organisation is
responsible for selecting participants from all regions of their respective countries. This balance promotes a comprehensive
intercultural exchange, allowing participants to gain a broader understanding of different European cultures and media
landscapes.
Participants will come from a variety of backgrounds: students of all levels and studies (communication, arts, social
sciences, and other disciplines), recent graduates, young professionals, youth activists, and NEET young adults. This
diversity of profiles enhances the learning experience as participants bring different perspectives, intercultural insights, and
skills to the workshop.
We will favour an intergenerational selection, i.e. a plurality of participant ages. This range ensures that young participants
and those who are older or have more experience (professional, academic, or other) can learn from each other, fostering a
dynamic, intercultural, and engaging environment. Therefore, the age range of the project for the participants will be 18-30
and minimum 18 for the group leaders.
A strong emphasis will be set on gender balance. We will ensure that in every group, a balanced number of male and female
participants will participate and we will communicate this to every partner.
Participants are selected through partner organisations in a democratic process, by open calls. The selection criteria will be
based interest in media and journalism and motivation to learn and actively participate in the project. Moreover, groups are
formed to ensure diversity within each team, mixing participants with varying levels of experience, cultural backgrounds,
countries, and fields of study. This approach promotes the most effective learning for the participants.
Importantly, we will include 2 participants with fewer opportunities in each group. Participants with fewer opportunities will
have an advantage in the selection process. The project prioritizes the inclusion of participants with fewer opportunities, at 2
young people per group facing socio-economic, geographical, or other barriers. These participants are integrated
seamlessly into the groups without highlighting their backgrounds. Upon request, travel tickets and any special needs, such
as medication or special diets, will be addressed by our organization to facilitate their participation. As well, we will ask them

Please describe the role and involvement of the participants from each participating group in all phases (planning before
submitting application, preparation, implementation of activities and follow-up).
We will involve participants in every phase of the project.
Planning: During the planning phase, our association conducted a needs assessment among our members and volunteers
to determine which topics they would like to engage with, not only as participants but also in organizing. Media was the topic
that many expressed interest in, which became the foundation of this exchange. The project was written with the help and
input of our members, and several young people from our organization will take part in the organization as well.
Preparations: Participants will also take an active role in the preparations. On one hand, they will have practical tasks, such
as planning their own travel and purchasing their tickets. On the other hand, partner organizations will hold preparatory
online meetings with participants from their country, and we expect all participants to join. Additionally, participants will need
to prepare for their intercultural evening, presenting their country in an exciting way to others. We will also send them articles
and videos in the WhatsApp and Facebook groups to introduce them to the project topics before it starts.
Implementation: Every activity has been designed for participants, focusing on their active involvement. In addition to their
active participation, we expect feedback, especially during reflection groups and discussions at the end of each activity.
There will be numerous opportunities for participants to share their opinions, even anonymously. We will incorporate this
feedback into the next day’s program and make necessary adjustments.
It's also important that participants will be involved in leading energizers, with volunteers sought for each session.
Furthermore, the activities will be highly interactive, giving everyone the chance to participate.
Follow-up: Participants will fill out evaluation surveys to assess the project and our work. They will complete their own
YouthPass, defining their learning process. Additionally, participants will be required to carry out dissemination activities.
The WhatsApp and Facebook groups will be converted into alumni groups, where we will encourage continued active
communication among the youth even after the project ends.

What are the basic elements of the activity? Please describe at the very least the venue(s), non formal learning methods
used, aims of the session etc.
The venue has been chosen as the Garden Panzió in Balatonkeresztúr. One of our team members has already participated
in a project there, not only inspecting the place but also staying for a week. Based on this experience, we believe it is the
perfect location.
The guesthouse is about a 20-minute walk from Lake Balaton, allowing us to provide a true Balaton summer experience for
participants. The accommodation can host up to 39 people. There will be no other guests besides us, which is a great
advantage, ensuring we don’t disturb anyone. The guesthouse is close to Balatonkeresztúr, offering access to all necessary
services, such as a store, pharmacy, and doctor, while still being near nature.
The venue also provides meals, which is a great relief for the organizers, as it takes the burden of meal planning off our
shoulders. The kitchen can accommodate gluten-free, lactose-free, vegan, and other dietary needs.
The accommodation has rooms for 3, 4, and 5 people. Participants will be mixed by nationality but sorted by gender. For the
participants' enjoyment, the guesthouse offers a swimming pool and a ping-pong table, as well as opportunity for sports. The
venue has large spaces where we can hold sessions, and there is also the option to conduct activities outside in the garden.

The venue is very easily accessible: there is a direct train from Budapest to Balatonmáriafürdő, making it reachable within
two hours, allowing participants arriving by plane to reach it easily, whether early in the morning or late at night. The

All the sessions are based on non-formal education and will be connected to media. The following is a list of the most
important activities of the youth exchange. The list doesn’t contain all the activities, however, all of them with a detailed
description can be found in the timetable.
Illustrate Your Introduction:
Participants will take part in an introduction game where they draw a word card and select an image to reveal something
about themselves, integrating elements of their native language and cultural experiences. This activity fosters a sense of
belonging and cultural exchange among participants.
Live news desk simulation
Participants will step into the shoes of presenters, reporters and producers. They are tasked with managing a flow of
incoming information, which includes prepared scenarios that simulate real reporting challenges. Participants will be divided
into groups, each of which will have to create a news flow (3 breaking news stories) on 3 different themes that they have
chosen.
Expectation & Commitment Tree:
In this group activity, participants express their motivations, expectations, and commitments. By visually mapping out their
thoughts, participants gain a deeper understanding of their own motivations and expectations and fears, fostering a sense of
community and shared purpose.
Self-made Code Names - Custom Media Edition:
Taking a leaf out of the Code Names game, participants create a similar game focus on the media industry. They generate
lists of media-related words independently, then collaborate in groups to expand these lists. After exchanging lists with other
teams, they play the game following standard rules, with teams guessing words based on clues from their spymaster. This
activity promotes teamwork, creativity, and knowledge of media terms.
Media Detective - Clues and cues:
Participants work in teams to investigate news snippets, images, and statements, honing their fact-checking and critical
analysis skills. Through collaborative research and discussion, participants learn to discern credible information from
misinformation, promoting media literacy and critical thinking.
The News Factory:
Inspired by the Undercover game, deepens participants understanding of media production complexities, focusing on
combating fake news. Through roles of journalists and saboteurs, it enhances critical thinking skills and awareness of
misinformation tactics.
Story Circle:
Participants build stories together based on given themes, enhancing their narrative skills and understanding of storytelling
structures. Through active participation and creative collaboration, participants learn to craft engaging narratives and
explore different storytelling techniques.
Role-playing game: reporting news:
Participants engage in role-play scenarios as interviewers and interviewees, practising effective questioning and ethical
journalism practices. Through simulated interviews and feedback sessions, participants develop their interviewing skills and
learn to navigate ethical dilemmas in journalism.
Mock Press Conference:
In this simulation, participants develop public speaking skills and learn to manage real-time information dissemination. By
assuming roles as spokespersons and journalists, participants practise effective communication under pressure and learn
to respond to challenging questions.

Project Design
Preparation, support and follow-up
How will you prepare the participants before the start of the activity (e.g. intercultural, linguistic, risk-prevention etc.) and
how will you support them during and after the activity?
Ahead of the project, we will hold a meeting with all the partners to discuss methodologies, selection process, possible risks,
logistics, responsibilities and go through the timetable, to make sure everyone is on the same page and has the whole
picture to avoid confusions, misunderstandings and to be able to effectively answer questions from potential participants.
Selection phase
The selection of the participants is part of the preparation process. This selection will be transparent and inclusive, and will
have 3 phases: (1) recruiting phase through a form that will be posted online (social media, partners’ websites, and other
platforms); (2) selection phase based on the answers of the form regarding specific criteria to ensure background diversity
(social origins, academic level, location, language proficiency …), age and gender balance of the group, as well as
participants’ motivation, interest in intercultural and media learning, and their willingness to engage in the activities ; (3)
informing phase, where participants will receive the infopack and all the necessary information after selection.
Preparing Participants Before the Start of the Project
Once selected, young people will help us every step of the way, from project planning and
decision-making to organising and guiding the workshops. This approach ensures that young people's perspectives and
needs are taken into account at every stage. This participation will take the form of a form sent 2 weeks before the
preparatory video meeting, which will take place 1 month before departure.
The form will be used to record the interests, wishes (for example, what they would want us to focus on regarding the
subject of the workshops) , fears, and experiences of the young people about the themes covered in the various workshops,
and the intercultural dimension of the exchange (language proficiency,, technical vocabulary, etc.), which will enable us to
adapt the content and approach of the workshops as best we can so as not to put the young people in difficulty.
The preparatory meeting will: (1) inform the participants about the results of the form, which will enable some of them,
among other things, to play down their situation and look forward to the project more calmly; (2) introduce the partner
organisations, as well as the team leaders who will present themselves, facilitating initial contacts and allowing the young
participants to become aware of the European dimension of the project and to practise their English by simply listening or
asking their questions; (3) provide an overview of the project (theme, objectives, types of activities, travel arrangements,
accommodation details, and emergency contacts...); (4) give instructions to the young participants (prepare a presentation
about themselves, their culture, their country, conduct some preliminary research on the different participating countries,
review English vocabulary...) so that they feel as comfortable as possible on the day of the event; (5) distribute an infopack
listing all logistic information regarding participants country (travel date and time, meeting points, mandatory documents,
contact details, accommodation, prohibited behaviour…)
During and After the Activities
First of all, to foster team building, communication and cohesion between participants, Ice Breaker Games related to media
will be set up. It is a fun and easy way to prevent stress. Moreover, to ensure effective communication and cultural
exchange, language support will be provided to participants with weaker English skills through pairing with stronger
speakers.
Second of all, all workshops are designed to give basic theoretical and practical knowledge on each topic to enable
participants with no previous experiences or knowledge on the topic to take part in it without stress. Besides, daily
“Reflection time” is planned at the end of each day to assess, evaluate and share one's feeling, experience and point of view
of the day’s workshops. The gathered feedback will help facilitators to take into account participants’ experiences and
adjust the upcoming workshops accordingly. Carefully listening to our participants is a sine qua non for the success of our
project.
What measures will you put in place to ensure the safety and protection of participants?
The safety and well-being of the participants are extremely important to us in every aspect.
Firstly, even before the project begins, the infopack will contain the most essential information participants may need
regarding their safety. We expect all participants to have a European Health Insurance Card and/or travel insurance. This
ensures that they will be taken care of in healthcare institutions in Hungary in case of any issues. If necessary, partners will
help participants obtain these. The infopack will also include the necessary phone numbers and safety-related information.
At the beginning of the youth exchange, to ensure everyone is aware of the rules applicable to them, we will hold a session
where, in addition to practical information, all relevant safety information will be shared. During this session, we will highlight,
among other things, moderate alcohol consumption, caution around and in the pool, as well as details about the venue.
Furthermore, we will jointly create and discuss the project rules, including points related to their health and safety.

Recognition of learning outcomes
How will you support participants to be aware of what they have learned and which competences they have developed or
improved? Please remember to include the methods that support reflection and documentation of the learning outcomes in
the daily timetable of each activity.
To ensure that participants are fully aware of what they have learned and the competences they have developed or
improved, the project incorporates a comprehensive and structured approach to reflection and documentation throughout
the project.
On the first day, participants are introduced to the Youthpass process and its competencies and how can they use it for
defining their learning process. Facilitators explain Youthpass's significance in documenting and recognizing learning
outcomes, setting the tone for daily reflection and emphasising the importance of self-assessment and competence
development.
Participants will set personal learning objectives related to media literacy, ethical journalism, and creative expression. The
initial "Expectation Tree" activity helps them visually map out motivations, expected outcomes, and fears using colour-coded
paper. Once settled, these objectives guide their growth and track progress throughout the workshop.
Each day concludes with a dedicated reflection session where participants evaluate their experiences and learning
outcomes. This reflection time is divided into three stages: (1)Individual Reflection (participants begin with individual
reflection, filling out a self-evaluation form while listening to calming music. This quiet time allows them to introspectively
consider what they have learned, challenges they faced, and their emotional responses to the day's activities) ; (2) Small
Group Discussions (following individual reflection, participants break into their national teams to share their insights. These
discussions focus on the day's experiences, what they found rewarding or challenging, and the skills they feel they have
developed. The national team leaders will take notes. (3) Team leaders meeting – after the small group discussions,
organizers will meet with team leaders to discuss the day, give feedback and communicate the needs of the participants.
Regarding the documentation of learning outcomes, throughout the different workshops (such as “Budapest Beat: Voices of
the City”, “Budapest Echoes: From Questions to Insights”, “Ethics in Media”...), participants create various materials such
as posters, videos, presentations, live simulations, interviews, articles, and a final booklet. These materials are documented
and compiled to showcase the learning outcomes and creative outputs of the participants.
The final booklet, summarising the project's key learning points, best practices, and participant insights, is created
collectively. This booklet serves as a tangible record of the workshop and will be posted on the SALTO platform. Sharing
this booklet on SALTO allows other NGOs and youth workers to learn from the project, thereby contributing to the broader
Erasmus+ community.
After the artistic exercise, the workshop transitions into a structured group discussion titled "Applying Insights to Daily Life."
Facilitators guide participants to reflect on how the skills and knowledge gained can be applied in their everyday
environments. This discussion is steered through targeted questions that help participants to consider practical applications
of their newly acquired skills, thereby reinforcing the workshop's impact and encouraging ongoing personal development.

project. The infopack will be prepared using Canva.
In the preparation phase, we will hold an online meeting with the partners and team leaders via the Zoom platform. In
addition, the partners will also organize preliminary online meetings with the participants.
Digital components will also stem from the project’s topic, as it focuses on media, making the use of digital tools essential.
For video creation during several activities, such as the Budapest program, participants will use their smartphones, and they
will also use their smartphones and laptops for video editing.

organizations will actively participate in this process, and Google Drive cloud storage will also be shared with participants for

Environmental friendly practices
Will you include sustainable and environmental-friendly practices in your activities?
Yes
Please describe them and mention how will you raise the awareness of participants on these sustainable practices?
We consider sustainability and green practices to be very important for an Erasmus+ youth exchange, and we are
implementing several measures to ensure this.
At the venue, we will introduce several practices to make the project as sustainable as possible: first of all, we will not use
single-use plastics during the project. To achieve this, we will introduce reusable cups, with each participant receiving one
that they can label with their name using a marker. This way, everyone will use only one cup throughout the project. Of
course, the accommodation will regularly clean these cups.
Selective waste collection will be available at the venue of the youth exchange. We will minimize food waste, and to reduce
the ecological footprint of meals, most of the ingredients will be sourced from local producers. We will also buy the
necessary items for coffee breaks based on this principle.

for recycled paper.

practical matters, where we will discuss actions the participants can take: we will highlight the importance of using the
reusable cups, turning off the lights and water when not needed, being mindful of food waste, and the importance of
recycling. We believe it’s crucial to educate participants on adopting environmentally conscious practices in their everyday
lives.
Additionally, three of our four partners, from Croatia, Poland and Romania, will use eco-friendly means of transportation.
This will help reduce the carbon footprint of the project, as buses and trains are significantly more environmentally friendly
than flying. It is also important to note that Hungarian participants will arrive at the venue by public transport.

Erasmus+ Youth Quality Standards
Organisations implementing Erasmus learning mobility activities must adhere to a set of quality standards. These standards
exist to ensure quality experience and learning outcomes for Erasmus participants, and to make sure that all organisations
receiving the Programme’s funding are contributing to its objectives.
I. POLICY PRIORITIES IN THE FIELD OF YOUTH :
Beneficiary organisations should contribute to the overall objectives of the EU Youth Strategy [1] and to the European Youth
Goals developed within this frame. They should apply the guiding principles laid down in the EU Youth Strategy.
II. BASIC PRINCIPLES OF THE ERASMUS PROGRAMME :
• Inclusion and diversity: beneficiary organisations must respect the principle of inclusion and diversity in all aspects
of their activities. Whenever possible, beneficiary organisations should open their activities to participants from
different backgrounds and with diverse abilities, actively engage and involve participants with fewer opportunities.
• Environmental sustainability and responsibility: beneficiary organisations must make efforts to design activities
in an environmentally friendly way and promote environmentally sustainable and responsible behaviour among its
participants.
• Virtual cooperation, virtual mobility and blended mobility: beneficiary organisations should integrate the use of
digital tools and learning methods to complement the physical mobility activities or to improve the cooperation with
partner organisations.
• Active participation in the network of Erasmus organisations: beneficiary organisations should seek to
become active members of the Erasmus network by taking part in activities organised by the National Agency or
other organisations. Experienced Erasmus beneficiaries should share their knowledge with organisations who have
no or little experience in the programme, providing them with advice, mentorship or other support. Beneficiary
organisations should also encourage their participants to take part in Erasmus community building activities.
III. QUALITY MANAGEMENT
• Responsibility: beneficiary organisations will be responsible for results and quality of the implemented activities,
regardless of the involvement of any other organisations or individuals. During the implementation of Erasmus
activities, beneficiary organisations must take responsibility for key decisions on all tasks that directly affect the
outcomes of the implemented activities, especially in relation to these quality standards. The core tasks include
financial management of the programme funds, contact with the National Agency, reporting on implemented
activities, as well as all decisions that directly affect the content, quality and results of the implemented activities.
• Transparency: beneficiary organisations may receive advice, assistance or services from other organisations or
individuals, as long as it keeps control of the content, quality and results of the implemented activities. If the
beneficiary organisations are using the programme funds to pay for such services, rights and obligations of both
parties (in particular tasks to be carried out, quality control mechanisms and consequences in case of poor or failed
delivery on part of the service provider) must be formally defined and available for review by the National Agency.
• Partnerships: While overall responsibility of the implementation lies within the beneficiary organisations, activities
should be planned, implemented and evaluated together with all partner organisations involved. Clear definition of
roles, and distribution of tasks should be set-up and agreed.
• Contributions paid by participants: as a form of co-funding, the beneficiary organisation may ask participants in
activities for contributions to pay for goods and services necessary for the implementation of those activities. The size
of the participants’ contributions must remain proportional to the grant awarded for the implementation of the activity,
must be clearly justified, collected on a non-profit basis, and may not create unfair barriers to participation (especially
concerning participants with fewer opportunities). Additional fees or other participant contributions cannot be
collected by other service providers chosen by the beneficiary organisation.
• Monitoring and evaluation: beneficiary organisations should put in place adequate plans and procedures to
monitor and evaluate the activities, to enable them to assess how they are progressing towards their objectives and
whether they have reached them. The monitoring and evaluation outcomes should lead to improvements in the
implementation of ongoing or future activities.
• Building capacity and integrating results of the activities in the organisation: beneficiary organisations
should take steps to use the results of the implemented activities in their regular activities, to build their capacity and
the capacity of their staff.

• Gathering and using participants’ feedback: beneficiary organisations should encourage the participants to fulfil
the standardised participant reports provided by the European Commission, and should make use of the feedback
provided by the participants to improve future activities.
IV. QUALITY LEARNING ACTIVITIES
This section sets the minimum standards to follow. Further guidance and tools are available to support the delivery of high
quality learning mobility activities, such as the Quality Framework for Learning Mobility.
• Practical arrangements: beneficiary organisations must ensure quality of practical and logistic arrangements
(travel, accommodation, insurance, safety, visa applications, social security, etc.). If these tasks are delegated to the
participants or another participating organisation, beneficiary organisations will remain ultimately responsible for
verifying their provision and quality.
• Health, safety and respect of applicable regulation: all activities must be organised with a high standard of
safety and protection for involved participants and must respect all applicable regulation (for example regarding
parental consent etc.). The beneficiary organisations must ensure that their participants have appropriate insurance
coverage, as defined by the general rules of the Programme and the applicable regulation.
• Selection of participants: participants must be selected through a transparent, fair and inclusive selection
procedure.
• Preparation and support of participants: participants must receive appropriate preparation in terms of practical,
cultural and safety aspects. They should be fully aware of what is expected from them before, during and after the
activity. Adequate guidance and support must be available during all phases of the activities.
• Support to participants with fewer opportunities: beneficiary organisations must ensure fair and equal
conditions for all participants, including those facing barriers (such as special learning needs or physical disability).
• Participatory approach: participants should - as far as possible - be actively involved in all phases of the activities
and receive adequate support to do so.
• Participatory methods: activities should be based on participatory methods and offer space for interaction of
participants, sharing of ideas, avoiding passive listening and allow them to contribute to the activities with their own
knowledge and skills, reversing the traditional roles of outside "experts".
• Linguistic support: beneficiary organisations must ensure appropriate language training, adapted to the needs of
the participants. Where appropriate, the beneficiary organisation should make maximum use of the specific tools and
funding provided by the Programme for this purpose.
• Definition and monitoring of learning outcomes: the expected learning outcomes must be clearly defined for
each participant or group of participants prior to the activity. Where relevant, participants should be involved in the
identification of the expected learning outcomes. Progress in the learning process should be monitored and adequate
support should be provided to participants in this regard.
• Identification and recognition of learning outcomes: non-formal and informal learning outcomes gained by
participants in the activities should be identified and documented, in particular through EU level recognition tools
such as Youthpass. Youthpass is a tool to support validation of non-formal and informal learning outcomes. It is a
part of the European Commission's strategy to foster the recognition of non-formal learning. Youthpass
V. Sharing results and knowledge about the programme
• Sharing results within the organisation: beneficiary organisations should make their participation in the Erasmus
programme widely known within the organisation and create opportunities for participants to share their mobility
experience with their peers.
• Sharing results with other organisations and the public: beneficiary organisations should share the results of
their activities with other organisations and the public.
• Publicly acknowledging European Union funding: beneficiary organisations should make their participation in
the Erasmus programme known in their community and in the wider public. Beneficiary organisations must also
inform all participants about the source of the fund.
